channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: 45 People, $200M Revenue. The Question Nobody's Asking About AI and Your Team Size.
executive_summary: In the AI era, organizations are facing a complex challenge regarding team size and productivity. Nate B Jones highlights that while AI can enhance output, it also raises coordination costs, making the five-person strike team the ideal structural unit for success. This shift is crucial for companies aiming to expand their ambitions rather than merely reducing headcount.
bullets: AI has raised coordination costs by the same order as output, meaning that while productivity may increase, the complexity of managing teams also grows. This is like trying to juggle more balls at once; the more you add, the harder it becomes to keep them all in the air.

The five-person strike team is identified as the ideal structural unit for the AI era. This is akin to a small, agile sports team where each player has a specific role, allowing for quick decision-making and adaptability on the field.

Scouts and strike teams serve different missions in the AI landscape. Scouts are like the advanced scouts in a military operation, gathering intelligence and paving the way for the main team to execute their strategy effectively.

Correctness-first thinking is essential for hiring and team building in the AI age. This approach emphasizes the importance of accuracy and reliability in outputs, similar to how a chef must ensure every ingredient is precisely measured for a perfect dish.

The real opportunity lies in expanding ambition, not shrinking headcount. Companies that focus on setting higher goals and aspirations will thrive, much like a tree that grows taller and stronger rather than one that simply sheds leaves to reduce its size.

AI agents and large language models (LLMs) have not solved the meetings problem; they have amplified an existing team size issue. It's like turning up the volume on a radio that already has static; the noise just becomes louder without improving clarity.

Dunbar's Number, which suggests that humans can maintain stable social relationships with about 150 people, has implications for team size. This is like a community where everyone knows each other; beyond a certain point, relationships become strained and communication falters.

The Harvard study mentioned shows that smaller teams are more effective in achieving goals. This is similar to how a small group of friends can plan a trip more efficiently than a large crowd trying to agree on every detail.

Peter Steinberger's solo agent model exemplifies the power of individual contributors in the AI space. Think of it like a skilled artisan who can create a masterpiece alone, rather than relying on a large workshop filled with less experienced hands.

The magic number for strike teams is five, as this size balances diverse skills with effective communication. It's like a well-tuned engine where each cylinder contributes to the overall performance without overwhelming the system.

The concept of the AI Slop Tax refers to the costs incurred from having weak links in a team. This is like a chain where one rusty link can compromise the strength of the entire structure, leading to potential failures.

To determine who is ready for the strike team model, leaders should test for high judgment and decision-making capabilities. This is similar to selecting players for a championship team based on their ability to perform under pressure.
key_takeaways: Restructure your teams around small, high-judgment groups of five. This approach will enhance coordination and decision-making, allowing your organization to tackle complex challenges more effectively. If you stick with larger teams, you risk losing agility and clarity in your operations.

Set a rule that at least 50% of your evaluation tests must check for correctness, not just functionality. Most teams currently have this split backwards at 80/20, which can lead to costly errors down the line. Ignoring this could result in a product that works but is riddled with inaccuracies.

Focus on expanding your team's ambitions rather than just reducing headcount. By encouraging your team to aim higher, you can unlock new opportunities and innovations. If you only concentrate on cutting costs, you may stifle creativity and growth.

technical_terms.Dunbar's Number: The theoretical limit to the number of stable relationships a person can maintain, estimated at around 150. Think of it like a social network where too many connections can lead to chaos and miscommunication.
technical_terms.Strike Team: A small, agile group of individuals, typically around five, focused on achieving specific goals efficiently. Like a specialized task force that can respond quickly to challenges without the bureaucratic delays of larger teams.
technical_terms.Correctness-First Thinking: An approach that prioritizes accuracy and reliability in outputs over sheer volume or speed. Think of it like a meticulous artist who ensures every brushstroke is perfect before moving on to the next part of the canvas.
technical_terms.AI Slop Tax: The hidden costs associated with having weak links in a team, which can lead to inefficiencies and errors. Like a chain where one weak link can cause the whole structure to fail under pressure.
technical_terms.Solo Agent Model: A concept where individual contributors operate independently to achieve results, often in the context of AI. Think of it like a lone wolf who can navigate challenges on their own without needing a pack.
